, · CURRENT AFFAIR-S. 
-The Eloquent Mr. Birrell had a hard task to do and 
he did it with only partial success; after all, in · justice 
to the ability of that .gentleman, it- is hard to be either 
eloquent or -humorous when the job in hand is to proller 
a. ha'penny stale and dam~gcd bun to people who want 
and have more than a right to a full · fresh loaf. His 
speech was no~ very_ lucid, l?erJiaps it was meant _to 
cover the bun m a little prehmmary haze, so that its 
insignificant dimensions might not be too glaringly out-
lined before the disappointed-gaze of the Irish people. 
His reference to self-respect was in bad taste; indeed, • 
we would say it was !nsoleut, but where's the use ?f 
ignoring facts and· with tihe fact that the teachers 111 
Conference ro;e • to . their feet and cheered this British 
minister . only a few weeks ago, it is perhaps not to be 
wondered at that the Eloquent Mr. Birrell should refer 
to the lack of self-respect in Ireland. 
In another part of this issue our contributor 
"Chanel,". give_s his views on the Bill. 
He, like every 
otl1er N 11.t1onahst, agrees that the Bill is a miserable 
little affair. 
He has got hot over the matter and advo-
cates the throwing of the Bill back in the face of the 
" Liberals." That, no doubt, is what we all would like 
to do-some of us, no doubt, would like to put a good 
stone in the Bill as we threw it back, so that the 
"Liberals" might remember it. 
He suggests likewise 
what is in all our minds, that even if the country endea~ 
vourecl to make the best of the Bill that the Lords may 
throw it out. It would be a far clearer issue if we were 
offered the refusal of the Bill, but all we have to choose 
is whether the country will advise the Party to go on 
with it even though the Lords may pitch it out. 
If 
the people fling back the Bill in the face of the 
Liberals, what will happen? 
What are the chances 
that the Liberals will bring in a far better one 1 There 
is no question of any power that we may get under this 
Bill whittling down the claims of Ireland.. The Bill 
rnerely puts to us a. problem in· tactics. I,£ we ,think we 
will get more by .flinging it back, J>y all meap.s fling it; 
if not, cool prudence would suggest that we .endeavour 
to make the best of it . 
The chief thing for the people to do with reference to 
the proposal is to keep to common sense about it. The 
fact that 21 years · ago the then Liberal Government 
brought in a Home Rule Bill and that the Li"beral 
Party pledged themselves to Home Rule is very 
, interesting; but we didn't get Home Rule 21 years ago, 
and we have had to put in a bad 21 years since. 
The 
common sense of the matter seems to be summed up in 
the verb " to take." The country has been learning its 
lessons in the verb " to do " during the last seven years; 
. the ver-b " to take," when you have the chance, is also a 
useful verb. 
·If it is ·true that Chamberlain offered 
better terms in 1887-, then, in the light of subsequent 
happenings, it was a great pity we did not act on the 
verb " to take " at that time. 
The perfidy of the 
Li,beral Party is no reason why we should play the foci!. 
Ai burglar has · no -right t<;, rob you of a hundred 
sc,~ereigns, but if he had the money and you had not the 
power to take it back from him it would be foolish 
to refuse to negotiate an offer of 2s. 6d. in the £, reserv-
ing your right to take the balance or parts of it at such 
time as you might be able to do so. It is not much t.o 
the purpose eith<::r to say that the Tories would have 
given a better Bill. If there was a policy that might 
have been worked with the Tories three years ,ago-well, 
it wasn't worked, and· where's the use of crying over 
spilt milk 1 
It is easy enough to say that the Parliamentary Party 
·have not been able to squeeze much of a proposal-and 
the Bill so far is only a proposal-out of the " Liberal " 
Government, but where is the common sense of that ·sort 
of rather cheap talk 7 
No amount of evidence against the 
character of tl1e Parliamentary Party during the past 
20 years is of much value as a contribution to the ques-
tion-what should the country do now in relation to 
this proposed Council 7 • Mr. John Redmond is not Mr. 
Parnell, and to give him his due he never posed as such 
a· figure. 
The fact that the " Liberal " Party is a 
frau,d, so far as Iveland is concerned, may be taken as, 
accepted. 
The common sense of the matter is, should 
we take in 1907-if we can get it-a. new additional

Local Government Bill, or should we refuse to touch it 1 
WE! cannot see how refusing it will compel the Govern-
ment to offer a better one, and their majority is indepen-
dent of, the Irish Party. 
The proper S·i11111, Fc·in attitude is to take what we can 
get, and use it for all it is worth, and look for mo1·e. 
With the powers we have at present we c.ould do much 
tnore if we willed it. In the early days of the LEADER 
one of our great indictments against Nationali~t I~elancl 
was that it was clamouring for a Home Rule it chcl not 
possess the while it refused to work to the full such 
com1mratively considerable Home Rule that it Jrn,cl. 
The Bill, so far as it confers addiUoual powers 011 ~re-
land, invites an n,pvlica.tion of Irish enorgy to shapmg 
Irish destiny. 
If the Bill, 
amended 
~n 
many 
particulars, is 
the 
best 
that 
the 
Pal'liamentary 
Party and the 
forces 
the1 
represent 
can . ~et, 
and if, as a consequence of this register of 01~r poht1?al 
weakness, we had decided to have no more Insh Parha-
mentary Parties at Westminster, at least the common 
sense thing to do is to take the little, such as it is, that 
the Party have got before we dismiss them. No de-
monstration that the Party is weak, logically demands 
that we are to refuse to take the Party's mite. If a 
commercial compa.ny starts out having the confidence of 
the shareholders and, instead of paying a dividend, 
goes into baukruptcy, the shareholders, at least, are 
anxious to get as much salvage as they can. If the 
members have failed, at least let the country have as 
big a return for its money as possible before it dismisses 
-if it should elect to dismiss-them. 
The Bill is a poor thing. But if it is decided not to 
take it-what next? 
Wait another twenty years? 
Agitate? The Land League was formidable because it 
handled a vile economic evil that hit the agriculturist 
so acutely that it drove many men to des·peration. We 
• have not a similar leverage now for raising terror-
striking agitations. The farmers' grievances are partly 
redressed, and ordinary business people in the towns 
make indifferent heroes ; as for our working classes, who 
are so largely hitched on to English Trade Unions, they 
would rather go to Sheffield or to America in search of 
work than go to jail for Ireland, or risk their lives 
behind barricades. 
Where is the agitation to come 
from 1 The more power we have the easier it will be to 
raise effective agitation for further powers and· a taste 
of what we may call tantalising power, power in name 
hedged round with irritating restrictions will only feed 
and inflame Nationalist agitation for further power. 
Of course, it is easy to deceive ourselves, particularfy 
those of us in our teens or barely out of them, as to 
what great things we can do in the terror-striking line-
fighting England by doing without tea and smokes and 
that sort of thing; but England has a sense of humour 
and a sense of proportion, and so it is far easier, and 
has been in the past, for green people to deceive them-
selves than to deceive England. 
The development of 
real, as distinct from fanciful, Si1111i Fein is to use the 
power you have, take what you can get, and keep on 
ploughing in the ground and not in the air. 
No doubt Mr. Martyn's happy little 
party 
will 
attempt to make as much capital as it can against the 
hated Parliamentarians. 
However, if Mr. Martyn's 
party attempt fo do a sword dance on the prostrate 
bodies of Mr. Redmond and bis colleagues, as a con-
sequence of this proposed Bi1i, we see that the o:flicial 
organ of the Gaelic League is elated at the prospect 
offered by the Bill. It says:-" He would bo a bold 
man who would venture to prophesy that these pro-
posals are actually about to be carried into effect. 
One 
thing is certain: if they, or anything like them, reach 
the Statute Book, we shall be on the eve of the greatest 
and most beneficent revolution in the modern history of 
Ireland. 
THE scnooLs WILL BE ouns. 
The shadows 
of Death and the Nightmare Death-in-Life will have 
passed away from the Irish landscape. 
Would it not 
appear that there is work-mighty work, glorious work 
awaiting oul' hands d~ring the next few weeks 1 " 
,ve have read the argument that we can wait, as 
twenty years is nothing in the life of a nation. 
Cer-
tainly twenty years is not necessarily much in the life 
of a nation, but it is a great deal in the life of a rapidly-
dying country like Ireland. Ireland cannot wait. Half 
an hour is not much in the life of a man, but it is a 
great deal if lrn is bleeding profusely with nothing to 
hand to staunch the wound. 
. 
If there is not a statutory provision made that any 
saving on the police and the law will be credited to the 
Irish revenue, the natural tendency will be to keep 
up these charges as high as possible. 
Irishmen will 
argue-where is the use, from the Irish point of view, 
of saving half a million on the police in order to keep 
some men out of " jobs," one cannot say " work," and 
give " the Treasury " in Whitehall a present of the 
half a million saved? Could not a clause be put in the 
Bill estimating the cost of these departments, and 
enacting that any savings on that annual cost shall 
automat-ically go to swell the funds of the new Irish 
Treasury 1 
The clause in the Bill that " regard shall 
be had" to savings in Irish expenditure in fixing :finan-
cial provision for future periods is worthless. 
There is 
not even a promise; and if there were, with all due 
respect to British Liberals and Tories alike, we would 
place no value whatever upon it. 
• But we think a strong effort should be made to get 
control of the police, and that the Council should have 
power to transfer existing policemen, whose services in 
the way of playing concertinas and creasing their poles 
might be regarded as not necessary in the nation-build-
ing economy, to other and more useful duties. 
The 
police force contains a large number of clever, adaptable 
and worthy men who would be only too glad to be trans-
ferred to other work. 
By this means the expenditure 
on police could be quickly reduced, and the men relieved 
from service could be utilised in many of the hundred 
and one directions in which useful work could be done 
in Ireland. 
The efficiency of the force could also, were 
. it under the direction of the Council, be instantly i>J-
cr\'lased by directing the police energies in the way oi 
suppressing real crime, collecting useful statistics, et<::. 
• ~Ve have read the proposed Bill through, but not 
bemg thought-readers we. do not profess that we under-
~tood _it all. 
We have no doubt there are a good many 
mgemously-constructed and tricky pit,s in it that caused 
t,~e drafters to smile as they said-" The green patriots 
will never see through the true inwardness of these little 
quibbling clauses." 
We would be as suspicious. of every 
doubtful clause in this Bill as a buyer sometimes is of 
a horse-seller. 
The nominated members ought to be done away with 
altogether. 
They are a British insult to Ireland and 
coming fr~m a ',' Libe\al" Government they are absurd 
as well as msultmg. 
'Ihey are, we take it, to be created 
for t~e pmpose of shielding the Protestants from the 
rapacity and tyranny of the " Idolators !" That's all 
~h~," Iclolators" get for their tolerance-proving! 
It 
1s 
all creeds, all classes," on the platform, and in the 
Press, when the Papists are to be induced to act gener-
ously; when even a petty Bill concerning some aspects of

local administration is concerned, then it is not " all 
cre~ds,. all ~lasses," but a 24 nominated contingent. The 
• ~momsts would get their fair share of representation 
m a Council wholly elected, and it is preposterous in 
those days of democracy, or talk of it to attempt this 
plan of 24 nominated members of the Council. 
I 
• 
The nominated members on the proposed Coun~il are 
a. so:p to Protest~nt Ascendancy, and many of the people 
who strongly obJect to the sop, and abuse the "Liberal" 
Governmen~ on that head, are not those upon whom we 
could ?Ot!nt in our almost single-handed fight these years 
past for Tolerance, Justice and Fair Play. If you fight 
for Tolerance, and beard bigotry in its den, as we did, 
you are met wit-h " all creeds all classes " talk from 
papers like the " Ha'penny Dr~adful ;" you are told by 
some "extreme" Nationalists that Orangemen are your 
fellow-countrymen, and. a " United Ireland " made up 
of the Irish and the anti-Trish is flung at your head. 
Not all those who don't like the nominated 111ember:1· 
proposal can say, as we at least can say, that we took 
off our coats and fought directly, and to its face, Lije 
bigotry and the Ascendancy that are responsible for 
suggesting them. 
• 
There is a very silly clause that " no preference sl1all 
be shown to any religious denomination." Who, in the 
name of common sense, · is to decide whether a prefer-
ence is shown or not? When we raised the question of 
exclusion becaus_e of religious belief in connection with 
t-he G. S. and \V. Railway, a certain number lifted up 
the whites of their impartial eyes in horror, though sub-
sequently they practically admitted our charge-which 
was palpably true on the face of it-by partly adopting 
competitive examination for their clerkships. 
The new 
Council are to take over the powers 
6of the National 
Commissioners of Education. 
Supposing a manager of 
a school in a Presbyterian district refuses to engage an 
" Idolatrous" teacher in his practically Presbyterian 
school, is the appointment to be annulled? 
And the 
man charged with bringing in this Bill with this clause 
in it is supposed to be a humorist! 
The right of the Lord Lieutenant to snap his fingers 
at the Council, and take the power into bis own bands, 
is preposterous, an~ must be knocked out of the Bill. 
The pow'er of the Lord Lieutenant appointing Chair-
men of Committees must go overboard. 
There is 
nothing to be said in favour of it. The proposed addi-
tional members of the Education Committee should be 
selected by the Council, and not by the Lord Lieutenant. 
Suppose, for instance, when the Tories are in power that 
Lord Ashtown, the editor of the "Carrion Crow," were 
Lord Lieutenant, who would he elect on the Education 
Committee? 
We suppose Captain Wade-Thompson, 
Mike M'Carthy, the man Coote, of Tyrone, and a few 
others of that ilk. We think that the number of elected 
members, 82, should be strongly opposed. There should 
be at least as many elected members as there are Irish 
members at Westminster-minus, if they like, the two 
interlopers from " Trinity College." 
The 82 proposed 
arrangement appears too much like paving the w3:y for 
a " Liberal " redistribution of seats at " no far distant 
date." 
The " Ha'penny Dreadful " is rather amusing these 
days. It, the org~n ?f the In_tcrnati?nal _Dump, ~as grown 
green with patriotism agamst tlus Bill. It 1s papers 
like this misnamed " Independent,'' with their anti-
Irish Exhibition projects and their tame "one man's 
ha'penny is as good as another man'? ha'penny" 
policies, that keep . a brake on the Insh fight for 
national self-expression. 
The comments of some of the provincial papers are 
not too wise. 
One paper says:-" The remedy_ is in 
our people's own hands. 
They can s-'!lash and dnve t~e 
treacherous Liberals from powe1· Just as they did 
twenty-two years ago. 
. 
. 
. Ireland can get all much 
as the brave Boers got if she insists on flinging Birrell's 
wretched Bill back in bis· leering and jee1·ing face." 
This declaration, spiced as it is with a reference to the 
" hrave Boers," would, no doubt, draw loud and pro-
longed cheering from a green audience. 
Picture our 
De Wets and Botbas bringing England to its senses by 
flinging, not the contents of Long To-qis and thousands 
of rifles into British Tommies, but flinging " Birrell's 
wretched Bill back in his leering and jeering face!" 
The Bill has one thing, and an important thing to 
recommend it-the " carrion crow" party are frantically 
n.gainst it. 
When one is at the lowest_ depth of despon-
dency concerning the petty little Bill a good prescrip-
tion is-read the ranting Unionists on it. A dose of 
Unionist screeches helps to lift up one's spirits and one's 
cst-.imation of the measure. 
The Irish Times said: -
" In the next place, its adoption will be followed by 
Separation as surely as night follows day." 
And 
again:-" For all these reasons Irish Unionists will 
have absolutely nothing to say to it. If it had no 
other fault than that .it paves the way for Home Rule 
that fault alone would be fatal to it." And yet again : 
'' This insane and chaotic measure must never be allowed 
to become law. 
The forces of the Unionist Party 
in both Houses of Parliament must be employed in-
sistently and incessantly in exposing its absurdities and 
in resisting its assault on the peace of Ireland and on 
the integrity of the Empire." 
Those extracts are taken from one leading article in 
the "Trish" '1'.i·mes. We take the following from the 
leading article in the following clay's issue:-" The 
astute politicians in his Party have been quick to detect 
its potentialities as an instrument of Separation. They 
see that the Bill gives them control of enormous re-
sources, and power to use them for the paralysing of 
English authority in Ireland." "The Unionist Party 
must lose no time in organising a policy of resistance at 
every point to a measure of which the declared object 
is not to reform the government of Ireland, but to 
make Irish government impossible pending the full 
grant of a Home Rule Parliament." 
A course d 
such extracts from Arnott's Times is calculated to rouse 
one for the time being into a state bordering on enthu-
siasm for the Bill! 
The country is very much disappointed with the Bill • 
w<>ll, we can't say that we are. 
The battles of Ireland 
illust be fought out on the soil of Ireland by the Irish 
people. 
Ireland, of course, can trade and negotiate 
with other• peoples, and her representatives in the 
British House of Commons can exert whatever pressure 
they can to help Ireland in the work of nation-building. 
But the idea of England " giving Home Rule " to Ire-
land appears quite absurd in our eyes. 
Ireland, in the 
last analysis, must take Home Rule, and the shortest 
way home, if it is also the longest way round, is to use 
all the power we have, and take all we can get. 
Vve 
are not deceived by England or the Liberal Party, 
because we would never think of reposing trust in 
either, so far as Ireland's just rights-as Irishmen view . 
and unqerstand them-are concerned. 
Those people 
who have been deceived by this Bill had first of all, we 
submit, deceived themselves. 
'l'he new Council, were it established in an acceptably 
amended form, might do much as controller of Plun-
kett's Department. 
After all that Department called 
forth great energies in the country, and had the De-
partment been worked on better lines it might have 
done a very considerable amount of good. 
The control 
of the Department by an acceptable Council is worth 
taking ; it could be made an instrum~nt for t~e P;·omo~ 
tion of Irish industries. The e:!Iort m the d11·ect1on of 
standardising samples and such classes of work would 
surely be rendered all the more easy by a Central 
Council · the work of detecting and prosecuting frauds 
committed by selling as Irish manufactures in Ireland

articles that are not Irish manufacture might be looked
after—and this is a most important matter, as it con-
cerns vitally the keeping up of popular and sound senti-
ment in favour of Irish-manufactured goods. The ques-
tion of nationalising and rationalising primary and
“secondary education is a matter of vast importance from
every point of view, not least from the economic point
of view. Had Irish opinion been able to impress itself
effectively on the school programme seven years ago
and since, what a fine sturdy body of budding Irish
manhood, full of enthusiasm for Ireland, knowledge of
Ireland, and knowledge useful for their careers'in Ire-
land might not we have growing up to-day. The econo-
mic and national value of even seven years’ controlling
influence on the schools represent something—taken
separately—not to be cast aside in order to glut a
momentary temper arising from disappointment.

Two ex-waiters at the International affair called to our
offices one day last week. They were Irishmen, and had
been dismissed from the place.” On Tuesday night week—
the third night of the Exhibition—about two hundred
men and women employed in the catering department
were summarily dismissed. All these, we are informed,
are Irish. It appears that Lyons, of London, brought
over about four hundred hands for the catering busi-
ness, and apparently Humphrey’s Dump turning out
to be a Humphrey’s slump, about two hundred of the
local hands got their walking papers on the third night
of the show.

It appears to be a case of great and cruel hardship.
- The dismissed hands, we are informed, were engaged
on the 15th April. The girls found their own black
dresses, etc., paid their travelling expenses in those
cases where the girls employed came from the country,
paid registration fees, and then on Tuesday night they
were summarily dismissed with about seven shillings
pay in their pockets!

Of the four hundred servants left in the catering
department there are, we are informed, only about
seven Irishmen and about twenty Irishwomen. When
the dump proved a slump, and when ‘‘ sacking’’ became
the order of the day, the Irish were sacrificed.

This catering department at the Exhibition, by the
way, is a decided menace to city hotels, restaurants and
‘tobacconists. We understand that even when Hum-
phrey’s slump puts on a spurt, as no doubt it will on
an odd day, there is a potential capacity in the catering
department for dealing with 80,000 persons. This catering
department will draw a good deal of custom into Lyons’
net, and a good deal of tips into the imported waiters;
the unemployed Irish waiters and waitresses can watch
the switchback from the banks of the Dodder outside,

and the ‘owners, managers and employees of hotels,
restaurants and tobacco shops will have plenty of time
to study , the incidence of dumps, and to examine their
consciences. g 5

Probably some Dublin shopkeepers look forward to
doing some trade as a consequence of day trippers from
the country to the Exhibition. We fear that, like those
who expected much from the *“ Liberal ’ Government,
they will be disappointed. ~Whatever money the
trippers have will probably be spent in the bars, tea
rooms, tobacco stalls, and side shows, anq the Dub}in
shopkeepers will merely have the f:onsolatwu of seeing
crowds pass from the railway stations to the show.

Mr. Walter Long, M.T’., speaking at Preston, Eng-
land, on last Saturday night, said:—‘‘He had often
said that Englishmen, Scotsmen, and Welshmen wou!d
never stand the kind of tyranny which was exercised in
Treland.”” We quite agree; they are not as tame as
we are over on this side. A Catholic Ascendancy in
England, Scotland and Wales would not be allowed a
long life.

Under the heading  Furniture Decorations” a
reader in London writes us: — ‘4. Capa, I am an artist
who follows this branch of art in London, and, as far
as I can gather, there is a fair amount of decorated
furniture sold in Ireland during the course of the year,
which unfortunately is not executed in Ireland. I do
not know whether “this is true or not, and should be
glad if you could give me any information. In the
natural course of events a revival in art is bound to
follow in the trail of the Language Revival—in fact,
there are signs of it already. Now, men in this branch
of art are scarce, even in London, which I suppose is
the centre of the furniture trade, and I feel certain that
if anybody would be willing to finance me, I could do
well in Dublin. Selfishness is not my motive in writing
this letter, for T have no reason to be dissatisfied with
my position here; I am simply writing from the Sinn
Fein standpoint, for such a venture would benefit Ire-
land. I am also writing for your opinion, not for pub-
lication, but if you think fit to publish it so much the
better.”’

We have had an inquiry as to where, if anywhere, in
Ireland bone handles for knives and ivory handles are
made. We were not able to put our inquirer on the
track of the cave of any Dark Brothers who may be able
to execute this class of work. But if this meets the eye

. It is pleasant work to call attention to a new Irish
industry. The Irish Preserve Company, 60 South Mall,
Cork, have placed on the market a butter and milk pre-
servative, and the market for this commodity is
naturally a big one in a country where the dairying in-
dustry is so large. Under date March 27th, 1907, Mr.
T. Williams, F.C.S., M.S.C.I.,, Chemical Laboratory
and Assay Office, Produce Exchange Buildings, 8 Vic-
toria St., Liverpool, writes:—“T beg to certify that I
have analysed a bulk sample butter and milk preserva-
tive, submitted to me by the Irish Preserve Company of
Cork, and also determined by practical tests its preser-
vative power. The results of the trials allow of no
doubt whatsoever that this preparation fulfils to per-
fection the purposes in view, whilst from the analytical
position, it supplies a really harmless and genuine, most
concentrated and invaluable adjunct for an industrial
dairy.”

Our many readers who are familiar with the dairying
industry can form a shrewd opinion as to the total
amount of preservative that is used and imported into
this country, the general reader may be, however, sur-

rised to hear that it is computed that about from
£50,000 to £80,000 of this commodity is used annually
in Ireland, and this large supply has heretofore been
supplied from firms in London, Birmingham and Glas-
gow. Representatives of such firms go round Ireland at
two seasons of the year and book orders.

The dairy managers and butter factors have now an
opportunity of giving support to an Irish industry, and
likewise of advantaging themselves.  The users have
the guarantee that the Irish Preserve Company are re-
gistered users of the Irish trade mark; their registered
number is 0117. The Irish Preserve Company boldly

_ announce that their prices and terms are more favour-
able than those of foreign firms, so that in requesting
the support of Irish dairy managers and butter factors
their appeal is on unimpeachable business lines.
Samples will be sent post free; and the least that any
person in the Irish dairy industry can be expected to do
is to test a sample, and judge the merits- of the new
TIrish product for himself.

TO A CARRICN CROW.
Bird of hoarse melody, there is delight
In thy deep raucous voice—and laughter too.
Without thee humour were not : thy notes are few,
But then they summon visions from the night.

Of grim-eyed Eire’s sons upon hot search
Through drear ‘‘ disturbed areas ’’ wasteful wild,
For blood of martyrs loyal, lamblike mild.

A gruesome feast to thee upon thy perch!

Old crow ! why start in conscience-stricken fright,
When Cato-like a just man goes your way.

Who spite of caws discordant dares to say,

Your harsh notes are a slander on the light
Of dawning hopes when spiteful passions cease,
In days that crown six centuries with Ppeace.

P. E. M.

AIDS TO CLIMBING THE CLIFFS.

I N my article in last week’s LEADER, which had to be

written very hurriedly, I meant to have said more
about Father O’Leary’s little works,Mi1on Caing,
than that they give the student a thorough knowledge
of 1p and td, and to dwell upon the importance of
studying the idioms in his writings. Mion Cainc—
(Part I.) deals with those two subtle little Irish verbs
in a manner that makes their use quite clear.  The
definitions of 1 and ©d given on the first page give
the student a key to their use that never fails him.
They are, ““1p is a link between two substantives or
two moods; vda link between a substantive and one of
its moods.”” “‘ The predicate comes next to 1, the sub-
ject next to td.”” There’s the whole thing in the pro-
verbial nutshell. Then follow some sixty pages of sen-
tences exemplifying these rules, and give all the tenses
and forms of the two particles. You are not half way
through Part I. when you have a thorough grasp of the
whole question of 1y and ca.

All who have learned Irish know that the use of 1p
and cdis one of the stiffest ““ cliffs ’’ that confront the
O’Growneyite. He does not meet it until he comes to
Part III. of the Third Book, and by that time he is
saturated, so to speak, with cd. Dr. Henry does well
in'giving 1 such prominence in Part I. of his ‘ Hand-
book *’ series. With Father O’Leary’s two little rules
and the first three or four exercises for translation inte
Irish at the end of Dr. Henry’s ‘‘ Handbook,”’ Part I.
(varied by the teacher), I have known children to get
a thorough knowledge of all the tenses and forms ot
1 and c4 in a month.

Part II. of Mion Caint deals with one of the most
subtle and, at the same time, most fascinating things
that the student has to deal with—viz., the autonomous
or indefinite form of the verbs. Mion Cainc and an
ctotalp Peaoap’s little work, ‘‘ Irish Prose Composi-
tion,” another re-print from the LEaper, pulls the
climber over what I might call the ‘‘ autonomous cliff
very rapidly. Part ITI. deals with the idiomatic uses of
the Irish prepositions which Father O'Leary declares
in the beginning of the book are the most subtle and
difficult things one meets with in the study of the lan-
guage. They are also dealt with very ably and lucidly
by Dr. Sheehan in his Sean-Caint na noeipe. The
conjugations of the Irish verbs and the declensions of
the nouns and adjectives can be learned from the Chris-
tian Brothers’ Grammar in a fortnight by any person of
intelligence. ~ For the student who cannot consult a
good Irish speaker for pronunciation'I know of nothing
better than the chapter in Dr. Sheehan’s book on the
pronunciation of the vowels and consonants. By fol-
lowing his directions as to the position in which the lips
and tongue are placed in sounding broad and slender
consonants, particularly ¢ and the labials and dent.?.ls
the student can acquire an almost perfect pronuncia-
tion, Dr. Sheehan when in Ring made a special study
of the position which the different vocal organs of the
speakers assumed in producing broad and slender vowel
sounds. By imitating the speakers in this respect he
has himself acquired a beautiful blas, and in his book

Mav 18, 1907.

he gives the result of his observations in this connec-
tion. T would appeal to men like ‘“ Chanel ”” and Sean
: ::)1;\50 Mupcava to cease beating the air about superfluous

onants in the spelling of Irish, Italian vowel sounds,
ete., and devgte their time and ability to learning to
speak and write Irish on the lines adopted by successful
students of the language. Lot them come to Ring next
July, and hear Dr. Tenebry lecture on historical Irish
grammar, and they will lose some of their spelling
veform enthusiasm. All this talk about the elision of
““ superfluous *’ consonants and the making of a ‘‘ royal
x‘ogd” to Ivish reminds me of a lazy school-mate of
mine who used to say that he would learn Euclid all
right if they would throw out them bloomin’ defini-
tions and postulates, and simply leb us draw the lines
and angles, and prove their equality by measuring them
with a ruler.”

Why am I so keen in recommending Father O’Leary’s
writings to the student? Because they are bristling,
every page of them, with the idioms of the language and
the speech of the people, and that’s what the student
wants. ““ Mr. Dowling,’”’ the Latin professor in Canon
Sheehan’s ‘‘ Geoffrey Austin,”” when teaching Geoffrey
Latin composition used to say to Geoffrey after inspect-
ing his Latin prose exercise—‘‘ Now, Geoff, how would
Cicero say that? Here it is, my boy, and mnote it.”
Neither Geoffrey nor Mr. Dowling could consult the
great Roman orator in person, but they had his orations
and Latin compositions to guide them. To the student
of Irish who cannot consult an Irish speaker, and who
wants guidance on some point of idiom, my advice is—
see how would Father O’Leary or Dr. Sheehan say it.
You will find it in Seaona, Cpsop ’Oeamain, the
Irish translation of the Gospels, Asop’s Fables, or in
the Irish articles at the end of Sean-Caint na noeire
an Tdtaip Peaosn devotes a large part of his ““Irish
Prose Composition’’ to impressing on the student of Irish
the great importance of getting rid of the idioms of the
English language when speaking or writing Irish and
ascertaining how an Irish speaker would express the
idea in Irish. Cardinal Newman, in one of his beauti-
ful lectures delivered to the students of the Catholic
University dealing with Latin prose composition, says:
““In good composition the point which requires especial
attention is idiomatic propriety. Two nations may
have corresponding words for the same ideas, yet differ
altogether in their mode of using those words. s
An Englishman or a Frenchman would be almost unin-
telligible and altogether ridiculous to each other who
used the French or English words with the idioms or
peculiar uses of his own language.” : Yo

Hence the most exact acquaintance with dictionary
and grammar will utterly fail to teach a student to
write or compose (or speak). Something more is
wanted, viz., the knowledge of the wse of words and
constructions, and the knowledge of idiom. (Idea of
a University, pp. 362-3). I trust that friend
<« Chanel ”’ and the other O’Growneyites will start out
ab once to climb the ‘“ cliff ”’ by the path taken by those
who have succeeded in reaching the summit. Those on
top will be very willing to throw them a rope now and
.again if needed. And in the ascent let not our pessi-
mistic friends be discouraging their companions by
alarms that they are going to slip on some superfluous
%, or smash their face against an asperated 0. By
the way, is it not too bad that we cannot carry on a
.discussion of this kind without losing our temper and
.endeavouring to disparage and ridicule our opponent as
Sean Mac Muncava has been doxpg wxbh'Mr. P. J.

.0’Sullivan. ’Tis wonderful how a little patience, good
‘humour and the spirit of the Sermon on l;hq Mount
make smooth and easy of ascent the many cliffs that
_confront us during our sojourn in this gleann nanoeof.
Seagan O Floinn.

THE FACTS OF NATIONALITY.*

HE subsidiary title, ‘ The Fundamental Constitu-
tions of this Realm,” more accurately describes
the contents of a recently published pamphlet—pro-
perly, book—from the pen of a clever, but anonymous,
writer.* The greater part of the work is devoted to an
historical exposition of the ‘‘ King, Lords and Com-
mons ’’ theory ; not from 1782 to to-day, but from 1172
to 1782. Te retires even further back, to show that
the ‘“ Parliamentary institutions introduced into this
country from England”’ were but the natural outcome
of earlier Irish institutions. They had a common
origin, too.  Discussing the advent of IHenry II. he
argues that from the Anglo-Irish point of view the con-
nection between Ireland and England rests upon a Com-
pact, not upon a Conquest, as the English would have
it. That it was not a Conquest I have no doubt, but
I take leave to differ from the author as to the Com-
pact of the ‘ King, Lords and Commons of Ireland.”
‘““ A Norman Settlement ’’ seems to hit off the situation
hetter. We may well believe that as Cromwell ‘ was
the first Republican, so also was he the first Unionist.””
Gipeanna¢ Gigin himself admits that many of the Irish
would not accept the English Common Law, ‘‘ and for
centuries it was confined to those mentioned (followers
of Strongbow and later English comers), and a few old
Irish families who valued it chiefly as securing them jus-
tice in their intercourse with the Aunglo-Trish.”” Again,
in the Parliament convened by James I. in 1613, ““all
sections of the Irish people were thus for the first time
represented.’”” Judging by this, if we are to hold fast
to the ‘“ King, Lords and Commons’’ theory the less
said about its beginnings the better. The Irish Ire-
landers of those times did not wax enthusiastic over the
Compact ; the Plantagenets were not their choice. To
avoid jealousy, however, amongst the native Irish
Princes the Irish Crown was offered to Edward Bruce
of Scotland. By his acceptance the ténaip teacc tradi-
tion would be preserved, because the Scotch Princes
““ were traced by bardic tradition to the family of
Conn.” For the same reason, although he refused it,
James I. was offered the Irish Crown. In 1603 he was
acknowledged King of Ireland, ‘‘ not on account of his
English Crown, but in spite of it.”” On this trifling
accident the author bases his whole contention. ‘“Thus
it came about that for the first time in our history a
King of England was also the legitimate King of Ire-
land, and, moreover, that his descendant can be so at
the present day.” We must not be expected to take
this seriously. “‘ There we have the explanation of the-
unwavering loyalty of the Irish nation to the Stuart
Kings, whether reigning or in exile.”” Hardly; I
incline to the belief that our loyalty was the result of
previous religious persecution. Dealing with the events
of 1779 and after, we are told that the Volunteers
brought to a close ‘“ the age-long controversy as to the
sovereignty of the Kingdom,”’ and compelled the British
Government “ to admit the whole Irish case.”” We
have nothing to be proud of in that age-long contro-
versy of 600 years. Except during 1613-1692—a period
of 68 years, when we exclude the Cromwellian régime—
the historic Irish race were barred out, and had no in-
terest in the ‘‘ King, Lords and Commons.”’

On such like points the author builds up to the fact
that ‘“ The constitutional doctrine is centuries
old, and in the course of those centuries has never
varied.”  We can readily discern his purity of motive,
but for all practical purposes he has made the history
of Ircland once more a history of the Pale, and has
been compelled to dispel many dreams in order to
defend his thesis.

€ipeanna¢ Giginputs himself the question—What
is the test of political nationhood? and boldly lays it
down that ‘ & nation is a State possessed of sovereignty,
external and internal—internal as being independent of
all control from without, internal as being supreme over
all within.””  Consequently Irish nationalism in his

THE SCHOOL FOR SAINTS AGAIN.

OUR contributor castigates me with chastened
sorrow as one who would bring into parochial
libraries, ‘‘ virginibus puerisque,’’ a novel dealing with
the illicit love of the son of an apostate Dominican for
the daughter of an actress.  Probably some readers
would trouble only about the plot. I am more in-
terested in the philosophy of John Oliver Hobbes. After
all, the fictitious element of the novel is the story, and
the psychological analysis of motion and character is
the living force which gives it breath. i

In spite of your correspondent’s criticism, I still
think that the writer who thus describes the Benedic-
tion service is a novelist who ought to be read:—

A sacred banner was placed—as a veil—before the monstrance,
and a form of address, known as a Meditation, was given from
the pulpit by one of the Fathers. Ho had chosen for his subject,
on this occasion, the crowning of our Lord with Thorns. It was
not a sermon—but an appeal to the imagination of his listeners.
They took part in the trial before Pilate, they heard the words
of the Accused and His accusers, the shouts of the mob, the brutal
jests of the soldiery. The whole tragedy was cnacted before
their eyes: many wept: the hardest were moved by the recital
of woes so poignant and so faithful to the human heart. The rest
of the servico, as its commoncemont is similar to the Litany,
which, translated and adopted from tho Roman Breviary, is ono
of the chief beauties in the English Book of Common Prayer.
At its conclusion, the pricst, mantled with the veil, makes tho

PRIMARY SCHOOLS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT.

HAVE pointed out in general outline what the
rights of the Church are in education; further-
more, that the authentic exponents, judges, and de-
fenders of these rights in the varying circumstances of
different times and countries are the Bishops of the
different countries in due subjection to the Holy See.
Their decisions constitute the Canon Law which
Catholics are bound to obey. If the Catholics of this
country desire to know what their duty to-day is re-
garding education, they are to seek for it not amongst
the collection of the Canons of Dionysius Exiguus in the
6th century, or Mercator in the 9th, or amongst the
Decretals of Gregory IX. They must listen to the
living voice of the Church speaking to them through
their own Episcopacy, and disregard the suggestions and
teaching of unauthorised individuals, even though they
be Catholics.

But what do the Bishops of Ireland say about the
management of schools about which ‘‘Inquirer’’ is
deeply anxious? Here it is:—

As authoritative statements made recently in Parliament indicate
that the Government of the country contemplate serious changes in
our Systems of primary and secondary cducation, and as some
pronouncements made by individual Catholics would suggest that the
gravity of the issues involved and their true nature are not suffi-
ciently understood, we deem it our duty to make the following

statement : i e
“That we feel that any limitation or restriction of the control

which is now exercised by managers over the schools of the National
System of Education, would be so injurious to the religious in-
forests of our people, as to make it imperative on us to resist the
introduction of euch a measure, and, in ocase it were adopted, to
reconsider our whole position in relation to these schools.”

‘“Inquirer ”’ can hardly mistake the meaning, force
and authority of these words. In the present position
of affairs, it may be well to give more of this authorita-
tive statement issued from Maynooth nearly three years
ago:—

‘That as the power of appointment of the teachers in National
Schools is the principal guarantee that Catholic parents have that
tho education of their childron will bo placed in (rustworthy hands,
and as the reports of the Inspectors of National Schools concur in
stating that that power is, on the whole, well and judiciously em-
ployed by the clergy, we aro satisfied that on moral and religious,
as well as educational, grounds it would be disastrous to interfere
with it.

“ Inquirer ”’ is evidently a Catholic. Perhaps he
would favour the readers of the LEADER in his next
communication what he thinks of this pronouncement of
the united Hierarchy of Ireland. It is said that there
are a few Catholics in Ireland who think themselves
better judges of what conduces to the moral and re-
ligious well-being of Irish Catholics than the Irish
Bishops whom the Holy Ghost has placed over them.
Possibly ““ Inquirer ”’ may be one of these. If he be,
he may give us some little hint to that effect in his next.

* The Bishops’ statement continues:—
t thoro is not sufficient reason for tho adoption of extreme

They speak from experience. Perhaps ‘‘ Inquirer ’’

Would ‘“ Inquirer *’ kindly inform us, what his quali-

I believe, the Catholic priesthood and people of Ireland,
will attend rather to their authoritative direction than

Let us see what more the Bishops have to say on this
subject. Their statement was made in 1904, and what

“If the improvement of education is the object which the

necessity for such amalgamation does not exist, whether as re-

Whilst Mr. Healy-who, in my view, 
bas done the best action of his life in speaking out freely 
this time-has shown that even the number of " jobs " 
at the disposal of the Council will be very few. 
Looking over the matter calmly, and applying Mr. 
Redmond's test, I think we must come to the conclusion 
that we are being offered an instalment of between 2s. 6d . 
and ls. 3d. in the £1; the financial provisions, indeed, 
come very close to this. 
And, for my part, I refuse to 
believe that Irish influence in Parliament and out of it 
has fallen so low that we are driven to accept this instal-
ment. 
So far I have striven to speak dispassionately. 
But I feel an intense passion, that our leaders should 
have so ill-conducted the campaign and should now pro-
pose so disgraceful a peace. It is a complete betrayal 
of the living, a dishonour to the dead. O'Connell, Butt, 
Parnell, did they all fight for this and nothing more, 
the right to dictate school courses, subject to a veto, to 
appoint to a few unimportant jobs, to play with a little 
pocket-money in a pauper parish council 1 
It is not alone what we have not got~ it is what we 
are getting that is the worst part. 
We shall have a 
parliament like the Duma, with all the outwar~ 
show, with none of the powers, a parliament without 
funds, without freedom, without future. For, . in a 
moment of candour unpremeditated, even Balfour said 
it did not lead to Home Rule. 
The result must be as in 
Russia. 
Through its very impotence, the new body will 
dissolve into a confused strife of social democrats, social 
revolutionar~es, reactionaries, and the rest, incapable of 
reasonable action, when all their forces are absorbed in 
the contest for essential freedom. 
We shall have a fine 
parliament, a grand stage for the Sinn Feiner, a con-
stitutional bulwark something like the pier _at Sandy-
mount, where there is a magnificent pier, but unfortu-
nately no sea. 
A parliament without power, without 
money, without ministry; will prove soon enough that 
Irishmen are incapable of govemiug themselves. 
I 
have often advocated the 15s. compromise, real admini-
strative Home Rule, handing over the keys of the Castle, 
which this Bill leaves untouched; but there is a vast 
difference between administrative Home Rule and the 
administrative Humbug. . 
• 
What is ,vorse still, our dishonour will probably be in 
vain.

will prevent the 1mymeut even of the ls. 3d. in~talmen~, 
just as it has done before.

SUGGEST the above as a name for an Irish-Ireland 
. boys' paper should anyone be thinking of starting 
the hke. Last year when writincr in the LEADER about 
how to Irishise the schools, the "'idea of such a paper 
occurred to me. 
I had a feeling, however, that Irish 
Ireland had at the time about as many irons in the fire 
as she could well attend to. 
Aud in the matter of news-
papers especially, that she had more than she had 
readers in sufficient numbers for. 
I therefore said 
nothing about the matter. 
Now, however, that writers 
":ho seem to be s01~E:thing of experts in that particular 
hne express the opimon that there is room for an Irish 
as opposed to an English boys' paper in Ireland I am 
interested anew in the project. Let me set down' some-
thing as to what, from my experience, I should expect 
would be the probabilities ahead of a venture of the 
sort. 
In the first place I may say tha.t I know but little at 
first hand of juvenile city life or tastes in Ireland. 
Perhaps that is why I have never made the acquaint-
ance of the " Boys' Friend " or of the " Boys' Own 
Paper." I don't know if these papers have much of a 
circulation in country Ireland. They didn't seem to 
have in my day-that is if they were in existence at 
that time. 
Nor do I remember any paper of what I 
take to be their type coming to our village at all. 
I 
remember the "Shamrock," though, and the "Irish 
Fireside" with " Uncle Remus," and the strategy to 
which I used weekly resort to get hold of them before 
anybody else in the house. 
And, on the other hand, 
I remember the "Weekly Freeman" and "United 
Ireland," and the many nights I was turned out of 
doors for " caffiing " during the reading of the eloquent 
leading articles and speeches. If juvenile country Ire-
land be anything like what •it was in those d?,ys there 
ought to be not only a market, but a noble mission, for 
an Irish-Ireland boys' paper. 
This is, however, too off-hand a way to settle the 
question; I proceed to some more practical considera-
tions. 
Brian O'Kennedy sa,ys neatly that we " have got a 
plethora of papers of the 'something for everybody ' 
type." Aye, so we have, and there's the rub. 
Is the 
reason our newspaper proprietors go in for pleasing 
everybody • this-that they couldn't subsist otherwise 1 
The · reason why English · publishers can afford to flood 
the Irish market is largely the economic •one that their 
home market is so big they don't mind printing a ten 
thousand or two copies over and above their absolute 
requirements. In Ireland, however, where we are only 
by degrees learning to read, .an expensive paper to find 
purchasers in sufficie11t numbers for its support, must, 
I take it, make a sort of Cheap Jack of itself. Any-
how; 
that's 
what 
our 
big 
weeklies 
do. 
• The 
question is-seeing the pains our men's papers take 
to cater for boys as well as for men, would a boys' 
, paper, in order to subsist, have to similarly cater for 
men as well as for boys? Ireland, after all 
isn'.t a 
countr)'. i~ which you can safely speculate o~ merely 
theoretic hues. 
The country, and the people, too, have 
got many undevelo1Jed resources, but you never know 
how many disappointments are in store for you when 
you set to to develop any of them. It is all very well to 
talk of turning the Bog of Allen into gold, but perhaps 
it mightn't pay. 
Speaking without any expert knowledge or trade 
experience in the matter, I should say that a boys' 
paper in Ireland could only be run in connection with 
one or other of the big dailies. 
For one thing, I sup-
pose it couldn't count on making much revenue out of 
advertisements, and hence could hardly pay its way in 
the open market. 
For another thing, if started inde-
pendent of any other paper, the other papers or their 
satellites would be on its track immediately, and the 
end would not be far to seek. 
In the third place, the 
literary market and taste in Ireland being 
what 
they are, and in the groove in which they are, such a 
new venture as a boys' paper would likely want con-
siderable and long nursing to start with, and such nurs-
ing means capital. 
So that I should fancy the easiest 
way to come by having such a paper in Ireland, and 
having it soon, would be to induce some of the dailies 
-the "Freeman" for choice-though, indeed, mention 
of the "Freeman" in connection with a boys' paper calls 
up visions of Poo-Bah and the three little girls from 
school-to take up the matter. 
The worst of it is, 
though, that even if the " Freeman " did get out the 
paper the " Independent " and others would probably 
follow suit directly, and presently you would have more 
than sufficient papers to go round. 
Result-back to 
tl10 " something for everybody " type once more. 
When all this is said, however, I dare say the root 
reason why we haven't a boys' paper in Ireland so far 
is just the same reason why we hadn't the LEADER 
sooner-that we haven't the man or men to start it. 
Irish Ireland is the only part of Ireland that bothers 
about the boys or girls at all-for the boys' or girls' 
national good, at any rate-and Irish Ireland is too 
busy and serious, and, .from being busy and serious, has 
contracted too grave and reverend a demeanour to 
easily skip and tumble about on the floor with children. 
After all, it takes a nation with its nation-building done 
to properly look after its young folk. 
You must know 
Irish well before you can prattle to baby in it, and you 
must have Ireland ·well and out of danger before she 
,:an amuse her children as she .shoulrl 
And as-I sup• 
pose I may take it for granted that readers of the 
LEADER are a~ one on this--we would be better without 
a boys' paper in Ireland that wouldn't be worthily 
Irish-I fe,i,1· we had bet,ter not be too sanguine of seeing 
tl10 paper soon. 
Perhaps the way we will eventually 
get it will be by degrees, by process of gradually weed-
ing out the irrelevant somethings from the best of our 
" something for everybody" publications. 
I am glad to have a cheery word or two to say in con-
clusion. 
One is that I have reason to believe there is 
a project on foot, a~1ongst some of those perhaps best 
qualified in Ireland for the work, to get out something 
that may be easy of development into an Irish boys' 
paper. The other word is, that Irish Ireland iu the 
South has already on the marke~ ",<,\n loc11.&nn "-· 
a paper intended primarily for the youngsters of the 
Gaelic League.

Hospital for over three years, which therefore appeals with double
cenfi lence to its never failing Benefactors to support the innumerable
Patients, intern and extern who daily flock to it for Medical treat-
ment.

Theadvantages of the Hospital are not confined to Kingstown ; they
are liberally bestawed on the Sick Poor (without religious distinction)
of the adjoining Parishes.

Far away from friend and home, many poor victims of accident on
the Railway, Harbour, and elsewhere, owe their lives to the timely
aid happily afforded by St. Michael's Hospital.

The Institution receives no Government grant, and is not endowed.

The yearly expenditure, necessary for the support of the good work,
amounts to several hundred pounds; to meet this large sum the
Sisters have to depend on the voluntary Contributions and Bequests
of a generous public.

The Sisters therefore appeal to the good and charitable in general ;
and while, with confidence, expecting the help necessary to enable
them to carry on this great work of mercy, they beg to return their
most sincere thanks for the cordial good-will and kind co-operation
they huve met with during the past years from Benefactors of all
denominations.
